title: Breeding management for meat rabbits
subtitle: Pairing, timing, and colony structure for a small productive colony.
excerpt: A meat-rabbit colony of one buck and three or four does can produce 50 to 80 fryers a year on a smallholding. Getting the timing right between kindling, weaning, and re-mating determines how productive the colony is. This covers the standard re-mating schedule, buck management, and keeping records.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Rabbits Kept for Meat (2019). Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), rabbit management. Cornell Small Farms Programme, backyard rabbit production guide.

content.text: A productive meat-rabbit colony runs on a repeating cycle: mate, gestation, kindling, lactation, wean, mate again. The interval from mating to re-mating determines the number of litters per year.
is 31 to 32 days; a standard 35-day cycle (mate to mate) means six litters per year per
, each of six to eight kits. A slower 42-day cycle gives five litters at lower stress to the doe and better kit survival rates.
Always bring the doe to the

's cage, not the other way around. A doe introduced to her own space will fight with a buck that enters it; a doe placed in the buck's space is more likely to accept mounting quickly. Watch the first 10 minutes; if the doe flattens and the buck loses interest, remove her and try again the next day. A successful mating ends with the buck falling off to one side. Allow two confirmed matings before returning the doe.

The standard small-scale approach is to re-mate at 28 to 35 days after the previous kindling date (the day the kits are weaned). This gives the doe a two-to-four day rest between weaning and re-mating and produces five to six litters per year. Re-mating while the doe is still lactating (before day 28) is possible but increases kit mortality in subsequent litters.

Palpate the doe at 12 to 14 days after mating. Hold her firmly with one hand on the scruff, turn her on her back across your lap, and press gently along both sides of the abdomen just in front of the hind legs. Marble-sized firm rounded masses spaced 2 to 3 cm apart are foetuses. If you feel nothing, re-mate without delay. Palpation takes practice but is more reliable than waiting to see whether the doe pulls fur and accepts the nest box.

Tag each doe's cage with the mating date and the calculated kindling date (mating date plus 32 days). Record the number of kits born, the number alive at day 7, and the weaning date. This four-number record per litter takes 30 seconds and gives you the data to identify a poor-performing doe before she has had five unproductive litters.
